retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Chondrocytes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Chondrocytes are mainly responsible for the production of collagen and the extracellular matrix that will lead to the maintenance of cartilaginous tissues within joints. Initial cartilage is composed of the mesenchyme during the fifth week of development. The mesenchyme activates in areas of chondral development and condenses to make chondrification centers. The mesenchymal cells develop into prechondrocytes, which later become chondroblasts; chondroblasts secrete collagenous fibrils and extracellular matrix. Consequently, collagenous and elastic fibers are stored within the intercellular matrix. The type of matrix composed contributes to cartilaginous typecast; there are mainly three types of cartilage: Hyaline cartilage: This type of cartilage is the most widespread and is usually present within joints. Fibrocartilage: This type of cartilage is part of the intervertebral disc. Elastic cartilage: This type of cartilage is in the Eustachian tubes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Joints -- Structure and Function -- Cartilaginous Joints
retrieved_docs.content: Cartilaginous joints have bones attached by hyaline or fibrous cartilage. The joints are further classified as primary or secondary cartilaginous joints, depending on the cartilage type involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Osteoarthritis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In a healthy synovial joint, bone ends are linked by an elastic layer of articular hyaline cartilage. This layer mainly consists of water, type II collagen that maintains the structure, and proteoglycans that provide the cartilage with the ability to withstand compressive and shear forces.

retrieved_docs.title: Synovial Chondromatosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The hyaline cartilage is hypercellular, and the cells are often atypical. These atypical changes are multinucleated cells, myxoid degeneration of matrix, crowding of cell nuclei, and large nuclei. Almost two-thirds of these loose bodies will ossify through endochondral ossification, while the rest will be unossified. [25] The number of loose bodies varies anywhere from two or three loose bodies to several dozens. They present in varied sizes, and several loose bodies may combine to form larger bodies. The number of intraarticular lesions is greater in the primary type than in the secondary type. High levels of bone morphogenic protein have been found in the loose cartilaginous bodies and synovial tissue. Usually, in primary synovial chondromatosis, loose bodies are small, rounded, and uniform in size. Whereas in secondary synovial chondromatosis, loose bodies are more variable. The loose bodies may create pathological mechanical wear on joint surfaces and result in various types of erosion of articular surfaces. The process is classified as a benign neoplastic rather than a metaplastic lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Cartilage -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Several types of cartilage are found in the human body, and their structure and relevant function depend on this variation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Cartilage -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Cartilage is formed from the mesoderm germ layer by the process known as chondrogenesis. [5] Mesenchyme differentiates into chondroblasts, which are the cells that secrete the major components of the extracellular matrix—the most important components for cartilage formation are aggrecan and type II collagen (see Image. Cartilage). Once initial chondrification occurs, the immature cartilage grows mainly by developing into a more mature state since it cannot grow by mitosis. There is minimal cell division in cartilage; therefore, the size and mass of cartilage do not change significantly after initial chondrification.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Function -- Types of Cells
retrieved_docs.content: Cartilage cells contribute to cartilage formation and maintenance. These cells are classified into chondrocytes, chondroblasts, and chondroclasts. Blood cells encompass a variety of types, including platelets and red (RBCs) and white (WBCs) blood cells, such as neutrophils and basophils. Blood cells are critical to oxygen and nutrient delivery, immunity, and tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Histopathology -- Chondrosarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: The production of hyaline cartilage characterizes chondrosarcomas to form a cartilaginous matrix. Lobules of cartilage are seen with significant variation in dimension. Cell nuclei show pleomorphism with chondrocytes varying in size and shape. Conventional chondrosarcoma accounts for over 85% of all chondrosarcomas. Chondrosarcoma can be subcategorized into primary central (developing within the medullary canal) or secondary peripheral (developing from the surface of the bone secondary to pre-existing enchondroma or osteochondroma). Histologically, both primary central and secondary peripheral are alike. Grading is an essential process that allows for the prediction of clinical behavior. [5] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Joints -- Structure and Function -- Cartilaginous Joints
retrieved_docs.content: A symphysis, or secondary cartilaginous joint, contains fibrocartilage (see Image . Male Pelvis Anatomy). This cartilage type is thick and strong. Thus, symphyses have a remarkable ability to resist pulling and bending forces. However, a symphysis is still often classified as an amphiarthrosis, permitting limited movement despite fibrocartilage uniting adjacent bones.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Joints -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Joints, comprised of bones and connective tissue, derive from embryonic mesenchyme. Bones develop directly through intramembranous ossification or indirectly through endochondral ossification. Mesenchymal cells directly differentiate into bone-producing cells during intramembranous ossification. In contrast, endochondral ossification involves hyaline cartilage differentiation from mesenchyme before getting replaced by bone. The joint connective tissue arises from the mesenchymal cells between the developing bones.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Joints -- Structure and Function -- Cartilaginous Joints
retrieved_docs.content: A permanent synchondrosis does not ossify with age but retains its hyaline cartilage. Permanent synchondroses connect bones without allowing any movement, similar to a synarthrosis. These joints are commonly found in the thoracic cage. For instance, the 1st sternocostal joint unites the 1st rib to the manubrium by its costal cartilage. Other examples include the connections between the anterior ends of the remaining 11 ribs and their respective costal cartilages. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Osteoarthritis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Early in osteoarthritis, loss of proteoglycans disrupts the cartilage fibers, leads to the softening of cartilage, and increases the water content. The earliest sign of cartilage damage observed by light microscopy is chondral fibrillation, followed by cracks and deeper fissures. Cartilage loss promotes chondrocyte proliferation and activation. Fibroblasts in, a type I collagen matrix fills the areas that have lost cartilage and are called a “pannus.” The remodeled matrix can then become calcified and invaded by small vessels later in the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Cartilage -- Structure and Function -- Hyaline Cartilage
retrieved_docs.content: Hyaline cartilage is the most copious type of cartilage in the human body. [1] It is pale blue-white and smooth to the touch. It is primarily composed of type II collagen and proteoglycans. The surface is usually moist, but the cartilage becomes dry, thinner, and more yellow with age. Hyaline cartilage is usually found in the trachea, nose, epiphyseal growth plate, sternum, and ventral segments of the ribs. Hyaline cartilage produces a resilient surface with minimal friction. It also has an excellent ability to resist compressive forces at sites of bone articulation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Cartilage -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cartilage has many functions, including the ability to resist compressive forces, enhance bone resilience, and provide support in bony areas where flexibility is needed (see Image. Cartilage and Bone Illustration). The primary cell that makes cartilage is the chondrocyte, which resides within the lacunae. The cartilage matrix consists of fibrous tissue and various combinations of proteoglycans and glycosaminoglycans. Cartilage, once synthesized, lacks lymphatic or blood supply, and the movement of waste and nutrition is chiefly done via diffusion to and from adjacent tissues. Cartilage, like bone, is surrounded by a perichondrium-like fibrous membrane. This layer is not efficient at regenerating cartilage. Hence, its recovery is slow after injury. The lack of active blood flow is the major reason any injury to cartilage takes a long time to heal. Cartilage has no nerve innervation; hence, there is no sensation when injured or damaged. When cartilage is calcified, the chondrocytes die. This is followed by the replacement of cartilage with bone-like tissue. Unlike bone, cartilage does not have calcium in the matrix. Instead, it contains high amounts of chondroitin, which is the material that provides elasticity and flexibility.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Chondrocytes -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Chondrocytes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cartilage is solely composed of cells known as chondrocytes. Chondrocytes maintain the extracellular matrix (ECM) and produce the cartilage matrix. Surrounded by collagenous fibers, chondrocytes release substances to make cartilage strong yet flexible. In general, chondrocytes are found within intervertebral discs and in any form of articular cartilage (AC). Chondrocytes play a crucial role in maintaining homeostasis within the AC joints that provide cushioning in joint movements. Like cells within other specialized tissues, chondrocytes distance themselves from each other by the cartilage matrix. [1] Chondrocytes are also responsible for chondral repair; due to their reconstructive nature, they respond to outside trauma in case of tissue damage. Because of their healing capability against degenerative conditions, chondrocytes are under active research for implantation and other reconstructive procedures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary finding site is the joints (klouby). Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the concept of اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). In the context of surgical procedures, Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a subcategory (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures and it classifies the anatomical category “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage.” Incision and excision of joint structures also has a relationship with the joints (klouby). This integrated knowledge connects endocrine‑related arthropathy to non‑traumatic joint pathology, emphasizes its joint involvement, and outlines the hierarchical relationship between joint‑focused surgical interventions and the specific joint structures they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1866
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage” functions as a classification category that organizes the anatomical subdivisions referred to as “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage,” thereby grouping these related joint structures under a common descriptive heading.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on joints is a classification that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on joints.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2546
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage” is classified as a type of “Incision and excision of joint structures,” indicating that it falls under the broader category of surgical interventions that involve cutting and removing joint components. Additionally, this procedure specifically classifies the anatomical entities referred to as “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage,” delineating the particular joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage structures that are targeted during the incision and excision process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: Incision and excision of joint structures is the overarching surgical category that encompasses the more specific procedure called Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage. The Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is classified as a division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage, indicating that it involves separating these particular joint components. Moreover, Incision and excision of joint structures is linked to klouby, the term for joints, underscoring that both the general and specific procedures pertain to joint anatomy. This hierarchical and classificatory framework clarifies how the targeted division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage fits within the broader context of joint structure incision and excision, supporting precise procedural planning and documentation in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 796
retrieved_docs.content: connected to the metastasis concept (النقيلة), reinforcing its role as a metastatic disease. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical network in which a general metastatic cancer node (kanker; metastase) is nested under various primary and secondary sites (skin, bone, respiratory/intrathoracic organs, thymus, endocrine glands). The broader neoplasm categories (unspecified malignant bone/cartilage tumor and neoplasms of uncertain benignity/malignancy) are tied to metastatic potential, emphasizing the clinical importance of recognizing metastatic behavior across diverse tumor types and anatomical locations for accurate diagnosis, staging, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base describes a hierarchical classification of hematopoietic and immune system neoplasms in which “lymph‑node tumors” (ورم الغدد الليمفاوية) are a narrow concept linked as a child of “cancers of other and unspecified primary sites,” which themselves are a parent of broader categories such as “malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites.” These lymph‑node neoplasms are anatomically situated within the hematopoietic and lymphatic systems and are closely associated with the process “spread to lymph nodes” (انتشار إلى العقد الليمفاوية). “Spread to lymph nodes” is coded as “lymph‑node cancer” (سرطان العقد اللمفاوية), classified as a child of “secondary and unspecified malignant neoplasms of intrathoracic lymph nodes” and included in the “malignant blood‑tumor SMQ” group (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)). The broader “malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites” also relate to “unspecified malignant neoplasms of bone and joint cartilage” (Maligne

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are closely associated with joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي), indicating that a range of non‑injury‑related joint conditions fall under the broader category of joint disease. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing various non‑traumatic joint pathologies as part of the overall spectrum of اعتلال مفصلي for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone is described as having the morphology of ورم خبيث أولي (primary malignant tumor). In addition, سرطانة توتية is classified as a type of ورم خبيث أولي, indicating that it too represents a primary malignant tumor. Both the nasal bone neoplasm and سرطانة توتية therefore share the underlying categorization of primary malignant tumor, reflecting their common oncologic identity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 469
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified under other non‑traumatic joint disorders. This arthropathy has its primary finding site in the joints (klouby) and is linked to the broader concept of joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي). Recognizing that this condition maps to other non‑traumatic joint disorders and involves the joints helps clinicians understand how endocrine or metabolic disturbances can produce non‑traumatic arthropathies that belong to the overall spectrum of joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1854
retrieved_docs.content: The arthroscopic procedure of joint, not elsewhere classified is primarily mapped to the category of other operations on joint structures, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of surgical interventions targeting joint anatomy. In addition, this specific arthroscopic procedure is linked to the general concept of arthroscopy, from which it is derived, reflecting that it represents a particular application of arthroscopic techniques that is not captured elsewhere.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is a specific therapeutic intervention performed on joints. It is categorized under the broader group of other or therapeutic procedures on joints, indicating that it is one of several surgical options aimed at treating joint pathology. Additionally, Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is identified as a type of بضع المفصل, linking it to the general concept of joint removal procedures. This classification highlights its role within the spectrum of joint surgeries and underscores its relevance for clinicians selecting appropriate operative strategies for prosthetic complications or severe joint disease.
